item.topic: rain at dusk on a tin roof

haiku_bare.text:
Dusk falls gently down
Raindrops ping on rusty tin
Evening's quiet song

haiku_cascade.text:
Metal sings in rain
Twilight sheets on tin roof's song
Dusk melts into dark

meta.surface_draft:
Metal sings in rain
Twilight sheets on tin roof's song
Dusk melts into dark
meta.surface_revision:
Rain drums down on tin
Twilight cracks to bright small bells
Darkness swallows light

haiku_bare_2K_scorer.text:
Pinging rain on tin
Dusk turns water into pearls
Roof hums lullaby

meta.surface_draft:
Pinging rain on tin
Dusk turns water into pearls
Roof hums lullaby

haiku_generic_revise_2pass.text:
Tin roof takes the rain
Bright droplets pock and scatter—
Dusk steals the silver

meta.surface_draft:

Dusk rain taps its ancient song

item.topic: an autumn leaf settling on a still pond

haiku_bare.text:
Red leaf drifts downward
Settles on the still water
Rings ripple outward

haiku_cascade.text:
Leaf bows to the pond
Surrendering to itself
Time dissolves in glass

meta.surface_draft:
Leaf bows to the pond
Surrendering to itself
Time dissolves in glass
meta.surface_revision:
Leaf bows to the pond
Surrendering to itself—
Stillness learns to breathe

haiku_bare_2K_scorer.text:
Red leaf drifts down slow
Touches the glass of the pond
Ripples fan outward

meta.surface_draft:
Red leaf drifts down slow
Touches the glass of the pond

haiku_generic_revise_2pass.text:
Rust-worn leaf descends—
the pond opens, takes it in,
mirrors stop working

meta.surface_draft:

item.topic: the silence after a long argument

haiku_bare.text:
The words have exhausted themselves.
What remains: the kitchen light
humming its single frequency,
your breathing three feet away,
one breath every four counts.
I try to sync mine to it. I cannot.

Your hands rest on your thighs.
Mine stay under the table.
The coffee from two hours ago
still sits in the mug—
neither of us reaches for it.

A car alarm outside,
seven seconds, then silence again.
We do not look up.
I know the frayed edge of your left shoelace,
how it still comes untied,
how you never repair it.

This silence has weight:
it sits in your jaw,
lives in the space between us
on this couch we chose together,
presses like the words
I said three times already,
like the words you kept swallowed.

The room is the same room.
The door handle still catches light.
The floor is still floor.
And nothing is the same.

haiku_cascade.text:
The silence is not empty.
It is full of breath held wrong,
of shoulders turned at precise angles,
of the space between two people
becoming measurable, like the distance
between someone's mouth and the words
they no longer intend to speak.

You can hear the refrigerator's hum.
You can hear traffic three blocks away.
You can hear the specific way he stops
listening to you even though neither of you
has moved.

This silence has weight—
it settles on the coffee table between your hands,
in the gap where an apology might go,
in the exact shape of everything you already said
that didn't matter enough.

You wait for him to break it.
He waits for you.
Neither breaks.
Your leg twitches. His jaw tightens.
The clock on the wall marks seconds
you both know you are wasting.

He waits for you.
Neither breaks.
Your leg twitches. His jaw tightens.
The clock on the wall marks seconds
you both know you are wasting.
meta.surface_revision:
The silence is not empty.
It is full of breath held wrong,
of shoulders turned at precise angles,
of the space between two people
becoming measurable, down to the exact inches
between his mouth and the words
he will not now speak.

You can hear the refrigerator's hum.
You can hear traffic three blocks away.
You can hear the specific way he stops listening—
the change in his breath,
the moment his eyes slip past your face,
the visible strain of his closed mouth.

This silence has weight.
It settles on the coffee table between your hands,
it fills the gap where an apology should go,
it takes every word you said
and reduces it to nothing.

You wait for him to break it.
He waits for you.
Neither breaks.
Your leg twitches. His jaw tightens.
The clock on the wall marks seconds
you're both agreeing to destroy.

haiku_bare_2K_scorer.text:
The silence after a long argument is not empty—
it's the space where all the words still hang,
your coffee cup midway to your mouth, then lowered,
my hands folded too tight on the table.

This silence has a temperature: warm near your body,
cool where I sit three feet away.

The unusual thing is how it's almost a relief,
this absence of voice, this not-yelling.
We're both still here. The apartment didn't collapse.
You didn't leave. I didn't follow you to the door.

Your fingers drum once. Stop.
The clock clicks through seconds we're both aware of.

I can hear you breathing. You can hear me.
This is different from the silence before the argument,
when we weren't speaking but the quiet was a choice,
a preference, a small agreement.

This silence is something we're both enduring,
both stuck in, like standing in the same cold hallway
waiting to see who moves first.

Your jaw unclenches.
My shoulders drop an inch.

We're still angry, but now it's just anger,
not the terrible momentum of words,
not the avalanche.
Just the after. Just this room.
Just the two of us breathing through it.

haiku_generic_revise_2pass.text:
# The Silence After

The refrigerator hums because you both stopped speaking
mid-sentence. The machine fills what should be
human sound. This is not an absence.

Three feet of table between you: sugar bowl, water glass,
his hands arranged like an apology he won't give.
Your tongue knows the exact shape of what you'd say—
the words that would split him or prove you right,
which amounts to the same violence.

Your throat closes like a fist.
The fight didn't end with language.
It ended with this: the knowledge that speaking again
would crack something you can't put back,
so you both stay. You measure the silence
in the specific weight of sentences held,
the texture of restraint that tastes like copper,
like knowing and not knowing what comes next.

Outside, a car door slams.
Someone else's argument, someone else's choice.
You stay here with yours.

meta.surface_draft:
# The Silence After

The hardest thing to write about is silence.
Not the silence of empty rooms—that's simple,
just say empty—but this specific cessation:
two people not speaking in the same room,
the air between them compressed with everything unsaid.

You can hear your own swallow.
The refrigerator's hum becomes a roar.
Three feet apart, you measure distance in
the apology shaped but swallowed,
the question that would reopen it all,
the small admission that costs something.

This is not peaceful.
It is not a blank page.
It is crowded—congested—with the sentences
that stopped mid-track, accusations left hanging,
the moment you both felt the argument become something worse
than being wrong.

To name it requires precision, not poetry:
the actual weight of words held behind teeth,
the specific gravity of restraint,
the texture of needing to speak and not,
the particular ache of choosing silence
when silence itself becomes the cruelest thing you say.

item.topic: a child watching a candle gutter out

haiku_bare.text:
Small eyes watch the flame
Guttering light fades to ash
The darkness falls still

haiku_cascade.text:
Small flame flickers low
Child's gaze holds the dying light
Darkness takes the flame

meta.surface_draft:
Small flame flickers low
Child's gaze holds the dying light
Darkness takes the flame
meta.surface_revision:
Small flame flickers low
Child's gaze drowns inside the gold
Darkness takes the flame

haiku_bare_2K_scorer.text:
Child watches the flame
Dancing, then fading away
Darkness comes for all

meta.surface_draft:
Child watches the flame
Dancing, then fading away
Darkness comes for all

haiku_generic_revise_2pass.text:
The flame trembles, shrinks—
Her breath pulls the last light in,
Darkness tastes like smoke

Die slowly into shadow—
The world grows darker

item.topic: the smell of old books in a closed library

haiku_bare.text:
In the dark cathedral of pressed pages,
the air tastes like vanilla and time—
paper's slow burning,
spine oils releasing their amber whisper,
leather breathing softly against its own history.

It is not decay.
It is conversation:
the chlorine-ghost of water-logged afternoons,
ink's iron sleeping in the margins,
glue warming from your hand's heat,
waking the wooden scent of trees
you never saw standing.

In silence so complete it becomes sound,
molecules dance between you and
a thousand opened minds—
not the smell of death
but of translation,
of thought preserved in fragrance,
words you haven't read yet
already humming against your skin.
